title: Breaking News in 1776: How the Declaration of Independence Spread Across the Colonies
summary:
Imagine getting your news by word of mouth or a single printed sheet. That's how Americans first learned about the Declaration of Independence. A new book by historian Emily Sneff, *When the Declaration of Independence Was News*, reveals the fascinating and fragile journey of the document.

On July 4, 1776, the Continental Congress adopted the Declaration. That same day, Philadelphia printer John Dunlap produced the first copies—poster-sized sheets called broadsides. These were rushed to state assemblies, military officers, and local leaders. Newspapers reprinted the text, and communities held public readings in courthouses and churches. Many people responded with "huzzahs" (cheers) and tore down symbols of British monarchy. But Sneff notes that the Declaration "was malleable, easily combined with other pieces of information and misinformation, or overshadowed by other stories." Some people were uninterested or even disappointed; their private diaries and letters revealed anxiety about what independence would bring.

The first printed copies didn't include the signers' names. That changed in January 1777, when Baltimore printer Mary Katharine Goddard—the only woman printing under her own name in the new United States—produced a broadside listing the signers by state delegation. She capitalized religious words like "God" and "Divine Providence" and proudly printed her full name at the bottom.

Sneff's book traces different printings across the colonies and the Atlantic world. Many copies were lost to time; only about 125 broadsides from July 1776 survive today. The signed parchment we know now didn't even exist until July 19, and signing began on August 2. By then, most people had already learned about the Declaration through other means.

Today, the original parchment sits behind bulletproof glass at the National Archives in Washington, D.C. But back in 1776, it was just breaking news—spread through fragile networks, met with joy and fear, and never quite the same from one copy to the next.
why_it_matters: Understanding how the Declaration spread as news shows that even the most famous historical documents were once messy, mixed with rumors, and received differently by different people — a reminder that today's news, too, is shaped by how it travels and who controls the message.

keywords.explanation: The assembly of delegates from the American colonies that adopted the Declaration of Independence.
Large printed sheets used to quickly spread news in the 1700s.
The Philadelphia printer who produced the first official copies of the Declaration on July 4, 1776.
A female printer in Baltimore who published a version of the Declaration listing all the signers by state.
Easily changed or combined with other information, meaning the news of the Declaration was not fixed.
The U.S. government building in Washington, D.C., that stores the original signed Declaration of Independence.

questions.correct_answer: It was the first to include the signers' names

It was banned by the British
questions.correct_answer: It was 'malleable' and could be combined with misinformation

background_read: The Declaration of Independence was adopted by the Continental Congress (the revolutionary government of the 13 American colonies) on July 4, 1776. At the time, news traveled slowly by horseback, ship, and printed sheets called broadsides. John Dunlap's original printing is known as the 'Dunlap Broadside' and is extremely rare.

Mary Katharine Goddard, one of the first female publishers in America, ran the Maryland Journal and published her version of the Declaration in January 1777. Her inclusion of the signers' names by state helped solidify the document's authority.

The signed parchment most people think of today wasn't created until July 19, and the actual signing ceremony began on August 2, 1776. Many copies were lost over the centuries, and only about 125 Dunlap broadsides survive. The original signed parchment is now kept at the National Archives (the U.S. government's official record-keeper) under bulletproof glass.
Article_Structure: LEAD: Compares modern news distribution to 1776 — word of mouth and single printed sheets.
└─ SPECIFIC: 'A new book by historian Emily Sneff... shows the fragile journey.'
KEY EVENT: Continental Congress adopts Declaration; John Dunlap prints broadsides on July 4.
├─ HOW: Broadsides rushed to state assemblies, officers, leaders; newspapers reprint; public readings.
└─ REACTION: 'huzzahs', joy, tearing down monarchy symbols; but some anxiety and disinterest.
TWIST: First printed copies did NOT include signers' names.
└─ LATER: Mary Katharine Goddard prints broadside with signers by state delegation in Jan 1777.
EVIDENCE: Historian Sneff traces different printings; only ~125 broadsides survive.
└─ TIMELINE: Signed parchment didn't exist until July 19; signing began Aug 2.
CLOSING: Original parchment now at National Archives; in 1776 it was just breaking news.

perspectives.description: The rapid spread of the Declaration through broadsides and newspapers shows the power of print to unify a population around a common cause, giving ordinary colonists access to the core ideas of liberty.

Sneff emphasizes that the Declaration was 'malleable' and 'easily combined with other pieces of information and misinformation,' highlighting that even in 1776, news could be distorted or overshadowed by other stories, much like today's media landscape.

The story illustrates how news of major events often takes on a life of its own before official versions are established. The gap between July 4 and the signed parchment shows that history is often reconstructed after the fact.

With only 125 broadsides surviving, the article reminds us that physical copies of history are fragile. Digital preservation of today's news is equally important for future historians trying to understand our era.
